question:
11. \( a, b, c, d \) are distinct integers such that
\[
(x-a)(x-b)(x-c)(x-d)-4=0
\]
has an integral root \( r \). Show that \( 4 r=a+b+c+d \).
solution:
Solution. Since \( r \) is a root,
\[
(r-a)(r-b)(r-c)(r-d)=4
\]
and since \( a, b, c, d \) are distinct integers, \( r-a, r-b, r-c, r-d \) are distinct integers whose product is 4 . But the only set of four distinct integers whose product is 4 is the set \( \{1,-1,2,-2\} \). Hence
\[
(r-a)+(r-b)+(r-c)+(r-d)=1-1+2-2=0
\]
so
\[
4 r=a+b+c+d
\]

descriptive_long.question:
11. \( \mathrm{constanta}, \mathrm{constantb}, \mathrm{constantc}, \mathrm{constantd} \) are distinct integers such that
\[
(\mathrm{unknownx}-\mathrm{constanta})(\mathrm{unknownx}-\mathrm{constantb})(\mathrm{unknownx}-\mathrm{constantc})(\mathrm{unknownx}-\mathrm{constantd})-4=0
\]
has an integral root \( \mathrm{rootval} \). Show that \( 4 \mathrm{rootval}=\mathrm{constanta}+\mathrm{constantb}+\mathrm{constantc}+\mathrm{constantd} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Since \( \mathrm{rootval} \) is a root,
\[
(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constanta})(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantb})(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantc})(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantd})=4
\]
and since \( \mathrm{constanta}, \mathrm{constantb}, \mathrm{constantc}, \mathrm{constantd} \) are distinct integers, \( \mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constanta}, \mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantb}, \mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantc}, \mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantd} \) are distinct integers whose product is 4. But the only set of four distinct integers whose product is 4 is the set \( \{1,-1,2,-2\} \). Hence
\[
(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constanta})+(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantb})+(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantc})+(\mathrm{rootval}-\mathrm{constantd})=1-1+2-2=0
\]
so
\[
4 \mathrm{rootval}=\mathrm{constanta}+\mathrm{constantb}+\mathrm{constantc}+\mathrm{constantd}
\]
.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
11. \( suitcase, marigold, lighthouse, harmonica \) are distinct integers such that
\[
(pineapple-suitcase)(pineapple-marigold)(pineapple-lighthouse)(pineapple-harmonica)-4=0
\]
has an integral root \( trapezoid \). Show that \( 4 trapezoid=suitcase+marigold+lighthouse+harmonica \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Since \( trapezoid \) is a root,
\[
(trapezoid-suitcase)(trapezoid-marigold)(trapezoid-lighthouse)(trapezoid-harmonica)=4
\]
and since \( suitcase, marigold, lighthouse, harmonica \) are distinct integers, \( trapezoid-suitcase, trapezoid-marigold, trapezoid-lighthouse, trapezoid-harmonica \) are distinct integers whose product is 4 . But the only set of four distinct integers whose product is 4 is the set \{1,-1,2,-2\}. Hence
\[
(trapezoid-suitcase)+(trapezoid-marigold)+(trapezoid-lighthouse)+(trapezoid-harmonica)=1-1+2-2=0
\]
so
\[
4 trapezoid=suitcase+marigold+lighthouse+harmonica
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
11. \( variableone, variabletwo, variablethree, variablefour \) are distinct integers such that
\[
(constantval-variableone)(constantval-variabletwo)(constantval-variablethree)(constantval-variablefour)-4=0
\]
has an integral root \( leafpoint \). Show that \( 4 leafpoint=variableone+variabletwo+variablethree+variablefour \).
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. Since \( leafpoint \) is a root,
\[
(leafpoint-variableone)(leafpoint-variabletwo)(leafpoint-variablethree)(leafpoint-variablefour)=4
\]
and since \( variableone, variabletwo, variablethree, variablefour \) are distinct integers, \( leafpoint-variableone, leafpoint-variabletwo, leafpoint-variablethree, leafpoint-variablefour \) are distinct integers whose product is 4 . But the only set of four distinct integers whose product is 4 is the set \{1,-1,2,-2\}. Hence
\[
(leafpoint-variableone)+(leafpoint-variabletwo)+(leafpoint-variablethree)+(leafpoint-variablefour)=1-1+2-2=0
\]
so
\[
4 leafpoint=variableone+variabletwo+variablethree+variablefour
\]
>>>

garbled_string.question:
11. \( povxtrnea, uyarqinfp, ldwsojemn, tgkmbzcia \) are distinct integers such that
\[
(qzxwvtnp-povxtrnea)(qzxwvtnp-uyarqinfp)(qzxwvtnp-ldwsojemn)(qzxwvtnp-tgkmbzcia)-4=0
\]
has an integral root \( hjgrksla \). Show that \( 4 hjgrksla=povxtrnea+uyarqinfp+ldwsojemn+tgkmbzcia \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Since \( hjgrksla \) is a root,
\[
(hjgrksla-povxtrnea)(hjgrksla-uyarqinfp)(hjgrksla-ldwsojemn)(hjgrksla-tgkmbzcia)=4
\]
and since \( povxtrnea, uyarqinfp, ldwsojemn, tgkmbzcia \) are distinct integers, \( hjgrksla-povxtrnea, hjgrksla-uyarqinfp, hjgrksla-ldwsojemn, hjgrksla-tgkmbzcia \) are distinct integers whose product is 4. But the only set of four distinct integers whose product is 4 is the set \( \{1,-1,2,-2\} \). Hence
\[
(hjgrksla-povxtrnea)+(hjgrksla-uyarqinfp)+(hjgrksla-ldwsojemn)+(hjgrksla-tgkmbzcia)=1-1+2-2=0
\]
so
\[
4 hjgrksla=povxtrnea+uyarqinfp+ldwsojemn+tgkmbzcia
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  4 be an even positive integer and let  
a_1 , a_2 , \ldots  , a_n be n pair-wise distinct integers.  
Define  

  P_n(x) = (x - a_1)(x - a_2)\cdots (x - a_n) - 2^{\,n-2} .

Assume that P_n(x) possesses an integral root r; i.e.  

  (r - a_1)(r - a_2)\cdots (r - a_n) = 2^{\,n-2}.        (\star )

(a) Prove that necessarily n = 4.

(b) Show that, after a suitable re-ordering,

  { a_1 , a_2 , a_3 , a_4 } = { r - 2 , r - 1 , r + 1 , r + 2 } ,

and therefore a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4 = 4r.

(c) Conclude that for every even n \neq  4 the equation P_n(x)=0 has no integral solutions.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Structure of the factors (r - a_i).  
Because the right-hand side of (\star ) is a power of 2, every factor r - a_i is an integer whose prime divisors are all equal to 2; hence each factor can be written uniquely in the form  

  r - a_i = \varepsilon _i \cdot  2^{e_i},            \varepsilon _i \in  {-1, +1}, e_i \in  \mathbb{N}_0.        (1)

Since the a_i are distinct, the signed numbers \varepsilon _i\cdot 2^{e_i} are distinct as well.

Let S = {e_1, e_2, \ldots  , e_n}.  Relation (\star ) gives  

  \sum _{i=1}^{n} e_i = n - 2.                                     (2)

Step 2.  A lower bound for  \sum  e_i  when n \geq  6.  
Write n = 2m (m \geq  2).  Because the numbers in (1) are distinct, at most two factors can share the same absolute value (they would have opposite signs).  To minimise the sum of the exponents while keeping 2m distinct signed powers of two, we take

  \pm 1 ( exponent 0 ),  
  \pm 2 ( exponent 1 ),  
  \pm 4 ( exponent 2 ), \ldots  ,  
  \pm 2^{m-1} ( exponent m-1 ).

Hence the minimal possible value of the left-hand side of (2) is

  \Sigma _(min_)(n) = 2\cdot (0 + 1 + 2 + \ldots  + (m - 1))  
     = 2 \cdot  [ m(m - 1)/2 ] = m(m - 1).                     (3)

Step 3.  Impossibility when n \geq  6.  
For m \geq  3 (i.e. n \geq  6) we have

  \Sigma _(min_)(n) = m(m - 1) \geq  3\cdot 2 = 6 > 2m - 2 = n - 2,

contradicting (2).  Consequently (\star ) is impossible for n \geq  6.

Thus n can only be 4.  From now on put n = 4 (hence m = 2).

Step 4.  Determining the exponents when n = 4.  
Equation (2) now reads e_1 + e_2 + e_3 + e_4 = 2.  
Because the e_i are non-negative integers and the signed 2^{e_i} are distinct, the only possibility is

  {e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4} = {0, 0, 1, 1}.                           (4)

Step 5.  Determining the signs.  
To obtain a positive product 2^{2} = 4, we need an even number of negative factors.  
With the multiset (4) there are exactly two ways:

  { +1, -1, +2, -2 } or { +1, -1, -2, +2 }.

In either case the unordered set of factors is

  { -2, -1, 1, 2 }.                                           (5)

Step 6.  Expressing the a_i.  
From (1) and (5) we have

  { r - a_1 , r - a_2 , r - a_3 , r - a_4 } = { -2, -1, 1, 2 }.

Adding r to each element yields

  { a_1 , a_2 , a_3 , a_4 } = { r - 2, r - 1, r + 1, r + 2 }.    (6)

Step 7.  Conclusion for n = 4.  
Summing the four equalities in (6) gives

  a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4 = (r - 2) + (r - 1) + (r + 1) + (r + 2) = 4r.    (7)

Therefore every admissible quadruple consists of the four consecutive integers r - 2, r - 1, r + 1, r + 2 (in any order), and conversely any such quadruple indeed satisfies (\star ).  This completes parts (a) and (b).

Step 8.  Non-existence for the remaining even n.  
Part (a) already shows that no solution is possible when n \neq  4 (with n even).  Hence P_n(x)=0 has no integral roots for all even n \geq  6, proving part (c).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension / more variables  
   • The problem is extended from 4 unknown integers to an arbitrary even number n of unknowns.  
   • One must treat all n simultaneously and then single out the only viable case n = 4.

2. Additional constraints  
   • The constant term is now 2^{n–2}, forcing every factor to be a signed power of 2 and introducing exponent calculations.

3. More sophisticated structures  
   • The argument involves combinatorial optimisation (minimal sum of exponents) and parity considerations on signed powers of two.

4. Deeper theoretical requirements  
   • One needs to combine number-theoretic factor-structure analysis with inequalities in order to rule out large classes of n in a single stroke.

5. Multiple interacting concepts  
   • Distinctness of the aᵢ leads to uniqueness of signed powers of two;  
   • Prime-power factorisation controls the magnitude of exponents;  
   • A counting argument (Step 2) links combinatorics with Diophantine constraints;  
   • Only after this global obstruction is settled does one descend to the classical quartic situation and recover the original identity.

Because it demands an argument valid for arbitrarily large n, careful optimisation, and a two-tiered proof (first ruling out n ≥ 6, then characterising n = 4), this enhanced variant is far more intricate than the original problem, which needed only a short case-check on four small factors.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  4 be an even positive integer and let

Define

(b) Show that, after a suitable re-ordering,

and therefore a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4 = 4r.

Step 2.  A lower bound for  \sum  e_i  when n \geq  6.

  \pm 1 ( exponent 0 ),

Step 3.  Impossibility when n \geq  6.  
For m \geq  3 (i.e. n \geq  6) we have

Step 4.  Determining the exponents when n = 4.  
Equation (2) now reads e_1 + e_2 + e_3 + e_4 = 2.

Step 5.  Determining the signs.

  { +1, -1, +2, -2 } or { +1, -1, -2, +2 }.

In either case the unordered set of factors is

  { -2, -1, 1, 2 }.                                           (5)

Step 6.  Expressing the a_i.  
From (1) and (5) we have

  { r - a_1 , r - a_2 , r - a_3 , r - a_4 } = { -2, -1, 1, 2 }.

Adding r to each element yields

  { a_1 , a_2 , a_3 , a_4 } = { r - 2, r - 1, r + 1, r + 2 }.    (6)

Step 7.  Conclusion for n = 4.  
Summing the four equalities in (6) gives

  a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4 = (r - 2) + (r - 1) + (r + 1) + (r + 2) = 4r.    (7)

Part (a) already shows that no solution is possible when n \neq  4 (with n even).  Hence P_n(x)=0 has no integral roots for all even n \geq  6, proving part (c).
